concept.title: Limits & Continuity
body: A limit describes the value a function approaches as the input approaches some point: $$\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = L$$
Standard limits worth memorizing: $\lim_{x\to 0}\frac{\sin x}{x}=1$, $\lim_{x\to 0}\frac{\tan x}{x}=1$, $\lim_{x\to 0}\frac{e^x-1}{x}=1$, $\lim_{x\to 0}\frac{\ln(1+x)}{x}=1$.
A function $f(x)$ is continuous at $x=a$ if three conditions hold: $f(a)$ is defined, $\lim_{x\to a}f(x)$ exists, and $\lim_{x\to a}f(x) = f(a)$.
Indeterminate forms like $0/0$ or $\infty/\infty$ often require L'Hôpital's Rule or algebraic manipulation (factoring, rationalizing) to evaluate.
L'Hôpital's Rule: if $\lim f(x)/g(x)$ gives $0/0$ or $\infty/\infty$, then $$\lim \frac{f(x)}{g(x)} = \lim \frac{f'(x)}{g'(x)}$$

questions.question: Evaluate: $\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\sin x}{x}$
questions.explanation: This is a standard limit: $\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\sin x}{x} = 1$.

questions.question: Which of the following limits equal 1? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: All three equal 1 by standard limit results. $\lim_{x\to\infty}\frac{1}{x} = 0$.

concept.title: Differentiation Basics
body: The derivative measures the instantaneous rate of change of a function: $$f'(x) = \lim_{h\to 0}\frac{f(x+h)-f(x)}{h}$$
Power Rule: $\frac{d}{dx}x^n = nx^{n-1}$. This is the most commonly used differentiation rule.
Product Rule: $(uv)' = u'v + uv'$. Quotient Rule: $\left(\frac{u}{v}\right)' = \frac{u'v - uv'}{v^2}$.
Chain Rule: for a composite function $f(g(x))$, $\frac{d}{dx}f(g(x)) = f'(g(x))\cdot g'(x)$.
Critical points occur where $f'(x)=0$ or $f'(x)$ is undefined. These often correspond to local maxima, minima, or saddle points — found by analyzing the sign change of $f'(x)$ around the point.

concept.title: Matrices Basics
body: A matrix is a rectangular array of numbers arranged in rows and columns, used to represent linear transformations and systems of equations.

For a 2×2 matrix $A = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}$, the determinant is $$\det(A) = ad - bc$$ The determinant tells us whether the matrix is invertible (non-zero determinant) and represents the area scaling factor of the transformation.
A matrix $A$ is symmetric if $A = A^T$ (transpose equals itself). Symmetric matrices always have real eigenvalues (Spectral Theorem), but their determinant can be positive, negative, or zero.
Matrix multiplication is not commutative in general: $AB \neq BA$. However it is associative: $(AB)C = A(BC)$.
The inverse of a 2×2 matrix exists only if $\det(A) \neq 0$, given by $$A^{-1} = \frac{1}{\det(A)}\begin{pmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{pmatrix}$$

questions.question: Which of the following are properties of a symmetric matrix $A$? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: A symmetric matrix satisfies $A=A^T$ and always has real eigenvalues (spectral theorem). Determinant can be negative.
